While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and [[:en:Temple|shrines]] are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual [[:en:Archaeological excavation|excavation]] contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-[[:en:Pacific War|World War II]] Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka City]], but were thought absent in the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the [[:en:Akyū ruins|Agu site]] in [[:en:Hara, Nagano|Hara Village]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in [[:en:Oyabe, Toyama|Oyabe City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually [[:en:Gable roof|gabled]]) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] spanning [[:en:Izumi, Osaka|Izumi]] and [[:en:Izumiōtsu|Izumiotsu]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

 at the [[:en:Yoshinogari site|Yoshinogari site]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of [[:en:Building|architecture]] where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]], they are classified as a subtype of [[:en:Post_in_ground|Post in ground buildings]], and structures identified as [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and [[:en:Temple|shrines]] are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual [[:en:Archaeological excavation|excavation]] contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-[[:en:Pacific War|World War II]] Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka City]], but were thought absent in the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the [[:en:Akyū ruins|Agu site]] in [[:en:Hara, Nagano|Hara Village]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in [[:en:Oyabe, Toyama|Oyabe City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

* {{Cite book|lang=ja|author=Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|title=Excavation Survey Manual|chapter=Excavation of Structures|pages=117–224|publisher={{ill|Dōsei-sha|ja|同成社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsei-sha|1=Dōsei-sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11414650}}|series=Settlement Archaeology Survey, 2nd Edition|year=2013|date=2013-07-26|isbn=9784886215253|ref=harv}}

[[ja:高床建物]]

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
{{wikidata link|Q115055460}}
[[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高床建物]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高床建物]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q115055460]]
Wikidata name: Raised floor building
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:25, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Yoshinogari-iseki takayukashiki-souko.JPG|thumb|250px|right|A reconstructed {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}}
 at the [[:en:Yoshinogari site|Yoshinogari site]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of [[:en:Building|architecture]] where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]], they are classified as a subtype of [[:en:Post_in_ground|Post in ground buildings]], and structures identified as [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. In Japanese archaeology, this term distinguishes them from {{ill|pit-house|ja|竪穴建物|lt=pit-houses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}}, which have floors dug below ground level, and from {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, where the floor is at or slightly above ground level.

While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and [[:en:Temple|shrines]] are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual [[:en:Archaeological excavation|excavation]] contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-[[:en:Pacific War|World War II]] Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka City]], but were thought absent in the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the [[:en:Akyū ruins|Agu site]] in [[:en:Hara, Nagano|Hara Village]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in [[:en:Oyabe, Toyama|Oyabe City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually [[:en:Gable roof|gabled]]) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] spanning [[:en:Izumi, Osaka|Izumi]] and [[:en:Izumiōtsu|Izumiotsu]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}
== Notes ==
{{Reflist|group=Note}}

{{Reflist|2}}

Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual [[:en:Archaeological excavation|excavation]] contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-[[:en:Pacific War|World War II]] Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka City]], but were thought absent in the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the [[:en:Akyū ruins|Agu site]] in [[:en:Hara, Nagano|Hara Village]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in [[:en:Oyabe, Toyama|Oyabe City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually [[:en:Gable roof|gabled]]) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] spanning [[:en:Izumi, Osaka|Izumi]] and [[:en:Izumiōtsu|Izumiotsu]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

[[File:Yoshinogari-iseki takayukashiki-souko.JPG|thumb|250px|right|A reconstructed {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}}
 at the [[:en:Yoshinogari site|Yoshinogari site]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of [[:en:Building|architecture]] where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]], they are classified as a subtype of [[:en:Post_in_ground|Post in ground buildings]], and structures identified as [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. In Japanese archaeology, this term distinguishes them from {{ill|pit-house|ja|竪穴建物|lt=pit-houses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}}, which have floors dug below ground level, and from {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, where the floor is at or slightly above ground level.

While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and [[:en:Temple|shrines]] are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual [[:en:Archaeological excavation|excavation]] contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-[[:en:Pacific War|World War II]] Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka City]], but were thought absent in the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the [[:en:Akyū ruins|Agu site]] in [[:en:Hara, Nagano|Hara Village]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in [[:en:Oyabe, Toyama|Oyabe City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].]]

Wikidata name: Raised floor building
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:25, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Yoshinogari-iseki takayukashiki-souko.JPG|thumb|250px|right|A reconstructed {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}}
 at the [[:en:Yoshinogari site|Yoshinogari site]] in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]].]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of [[:en:Building|architecture]] where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]], they are classified as a subtype of [[:en:Post_in_ground|Post in ground buildings]], and structures identified as [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. In Japanese archaeology, this term distinguishes them from {{ill|pit-house|ja|竪穴建物|lt=pit-houses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}}, which have floors dug below ground level, and from {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, where the floor is at or slightly above ground level.

While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and [[:en:Stilt_house|Stilt houses]] are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and [[:en:Temple|shrines]] are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the [[:en:Agency for Cultural Affairs|Agency for Cultural Affairs]] since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual [[:en:Archaeological excavation|excavation]] contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-[[:en:Pacific War|World War II]] Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] in [[:en:Shizuoka (city)|Shizuoka City]], but were thought absent in the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the [[:en:Akyū ruins|Agu site]] in [[:en:Hara, Nagano|Hara Village]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in [[:en:Oyabe, Toyama|Oyabe City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]], uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually [[:en:Gable roof|gabled]]) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro site]] and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the [[:en:Ikegami-Sone Site|Ikegami-Sone Site]] spanning [[:en:Izumi, Osaka|Izumi]] and [[:en:Izumiōtsu|Izumiotsu]] in [[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]].{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=excavation}} contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-{{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War II}} Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka City}}, but were thought absent in the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the {{ill|Akyū ruins|en|Akyū ruins|WD=Q11657196|lt=Agu site}} in {{ill|Hara, Nagano|en|Hara, Nagano|WD=Q1203041|lt=Hara Village}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in {{ill|Oyabe, Toyama|en|Oyabe, Toyama|WD=Q734366|lt=Oyabe City}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}, uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled}}) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} spanning {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi}} and {{ill|Izumiōtsu|en|Izumiōtsu|WD=Q873553|lt=Izumiotsu}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

[[File:Yoshinogari-iseki takayukashiki-souko.JPG|thumb|250px|right|A reconstructed {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}}
 at the {{ill|Yoshinogari site|en|Yoshinogari site|WD=Q1049645|lt=Yoshinogari site}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}.]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of {{ill|Building|en|Building|WD=Q41176|lt=architecture}} where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology}}, they are classified as a subtype of {{ill|Post_in_ground|en|Post_in_ground|WD=Q1695743|lt=Post in ground buildings}}, and structures identified as {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses}} or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. In Japanese archaeology, this term distinguishes them from {{ill|pit-house|ja|竪穴建物|lt=pit-houses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}}, which have floors dug below ground level, and from {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, where the floor is at or slightly above ground level.

While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses}} are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=shrines}} are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=excavation}} contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-{{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War II}} Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka City}}, but were thought absent in the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the {{ill|Akyū ruins|en|Akyū ruins|WD=Q11657196|lt=Agu site}} in {{ill|Hara, Nagano|en|Hara, Nagano|WD=Q1203041|lt=Hara Village}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in {{ill|Oyabe, Toyama|en|Oyabe, Toyama|WD=Q734366|lt=Oyabe City}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}, uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled}}) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} spanning {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi}} and {{ill|Izumiōtsu|en|Izumiōtsu|WD=Q873553|lt=Izumiotsu}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of {{ill|Building|en|Building|WD=Q41176|lt=architecture}} where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology}}, they are classified as a subtype of {{ill|Post_in_ground|en|Post_in_ground|WD=Q1695743|lt=Post in ground buildings}}, and structures identified as {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses}} or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=excavation}} contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in {{ill|Oyabe, Toyama|en|Oyabe, Toyama|WD=Q734366|lt=Oyabe City}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}, uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled}}) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} spanning {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi}} and {{ill|Izumiōtsu|en|Izumiōtsu|WD=Q873553|lt=Izumiotsu}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高床建物]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高床建物]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高床建物]]


[[File:Yoshinogari-iseki takayukashiki-souko.JPG|thumb|250px|right|A reconstructed {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}}
 at the {{ill|Yoshinogari site|en|Yoshinogari site|WD=Q1049645|lt=Yoshinogari site}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture}}.]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of {{ill|Building|en|Building|WD=Q41176|lt=architecture}} where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology}}, they are classified as a subtype of {{ill|Post_in_ground|en|Post_in_ground|WD=Q1695743|lt=Post in ground buildings}}, and structures identified as {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses}} or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. In Japanese archaeology, this term distinguishes them from {{ill|pit-house|ja|竪穴建物|lt=pit-houses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}}, which have floors dug below ground level, and from {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, where the floor is at or slightly above ground level.

While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses}} are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=shrines}} are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=excavation}} contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-{{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War II}} Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka City}}, but were thought absent in the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the {{ill|Akyū ruins|en|Akyū ruins|WD=Q11657196|lt=Agu site}} in {{ill|Hara, Nagano|en|Hara, Nagano|WD=Q1203041|lt=Hara Village}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in {{ill|Oyabe, Toyama|en|Oyabe, Toyama|WD=Q734366|lt=Oyabe City}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}, uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled}}) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site}} and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site}} spanning {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi}} and {{ill|Izumiōtsu|en|Izumiōtsu|WD=Q873553|lt=Izumiotsu}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site|1=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高床建物]]


[[File:Yoshinogari-iseki takayukashiki-souko.JPG|thumb|250px|right|A reconstructed {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}}
 at the {{ill|Yoshinogari site|en|Yoshinogari site|WD=Q1049645|lt=Yoshinogari site|1=Yoshinogari site}} in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|en|Saga Prefecture|WD=Q160420|lt=Saga Prefecture|1=Saga Prefecture}}.]]

'''Raised-floor buildings''' (高床建物, *Takayuka-tatemono*) are a type of {{ill|Building|en|Building|WD=Q41176|lt=architecture|1=Building}} where the floor is constructed above ground level, with the space beneath the floor sometimes used as part of the living area. They are also known as '''raised-floor architecture''' (高床建築, *Takayuka-kenchiku*). In {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}}, they are classified as a subtype of {{ill|Post_in_ground|en|Post_in_ground|WD=Q1695743|lt=Post in ground buildings|1=Post in ground}}, and structures identified as {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses|1=Stilt house}} or {{ill|raised-floor warehouses|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouses|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} from archaeological sites fall under this category. Structures with floors slightly elevated but not usable as living space are distinguished as "lifted-floor buildings" (揚床建物, *Ageyuka-tatemono*).
== Overview ==

Raised-floor buildings are constructed by placing horizontal beams, known as *Ōbiki* and *Neda*, on top of vertical {{ill|postholes|ja|掘立柱|1=Postholes|2=ja|3=掘立柱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/postholes|qq=}} (support posts), with planks laid over them to form the floor above ground level. In Japanese archaeology, this term distinguishes them from {{ill|pit-house|ja|竪穴建物|lt=pit-houses|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-house|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}}, which have floors dug below ground level, and from {{ill|ground-level buildings|ja|平地建物|1=Flatland dwellings|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ground-level buildings|qq=}}, where the floor is at or slightly above ground level.

While {{ill|Raised floor building|ja|高床式倉庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|lt=Raised floor buildings}} and {{ill|Stilt_house|en|Stilt_house|WD=Q33565|lt=Stilt houses|1=Stilt house}} are well-known examples of ancient Japanese raised-floor structures, other forms like {{ill|watchtowers|ja|櫓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/watchtowers|1=yagura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11544657}} and {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=shrines|1=Temple}} are also assumed to have existed. Therefore, the term "raised-floor buildings" is now used comprehensively in archaeology to refer to posthole structures presumed to have been raised-floor constructions.<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|author={{ill|Buried Cultural Properties Center|ja|埋蔵文化財センター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buried Cultural Properties Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Buried cultural property center|WD=Q11425913}}|url=https://www.yoshinogari.jp/ym/episode04/house.html|title=1. Housing and Architecture|website={{ill|Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja|国営吉野ヶ里歴史公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshinogari Historical Park|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 吉野ヶ里遺跡|1=Yoshinogari site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1049645}}|access-date=2022-10-29}}</ref>

Similar to the shift from using "style" (式) in terms like "ground-level style dwellings" to simply "ground-level dwellings," the usage of "style" in "raised-floor style buildings" (高床式建物) has declined.{{Refnest|group=Note|In excavation manuals published by the {{ill|Agency for Cultural Affairs|en|Agency for Cultural Affairs|WD=Q1009519|lt=Agency for Cultural Affairs|1=Agency for Cultural Affairs}} since the 2010s, the term "style" has been omitted from these designations.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|pp=117–224}}}}

In actual {{ill|Archaeological excavation|en|Archaeological excavation|WD=Q959782|lt=excavation|1=Archaeological excavation}} contexts, most posthole building remains are identified only by posthole traces, with walls, roofs, or above-ground structures rarely preserved. However, the depth of postholes tends to correspond to the height of the original posts, with deeper postholes suggesting raised-floor buildings (like warehouses) and shallower ones indicating lifted-floor or ground-level structures.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}

In post-{{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=World War II|1=Pacific War}} Japanese archaeological history, raised-floor structures were known from {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site|1=Toro (archaeological site)}} in {{ill|Shizuoka (city)|en|Shizuoka (city)|WD=Q174691|lt=Shizuoka City|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, but were thought absent in the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}}. However, in 1977, posthole alignments suggesting raised-floor buildings were discovered at the {{ill|Akyū ruins|en|Akyū ruins|WD=Q11657196|lt=Agu site|1=Akyū ruins}} in {{ill|Hara, Nagano|en|Hara, Nagano|WD=Q1203041|lt=Hara Village|1=Hara, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, though initially identified as "rectangular pit groups." In 1988, excavations at the {{ill|Sakuramachi site|ja|桜町遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakuramachi site|1=Sakuramachi site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538176}} in {{ill|Oyabe, Toyama|en|Oyabe, Toyama|WD=Q734366|lt=Oyabe City|1=Oyabe, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}, uncovered post materials with mortise holes, confirming that raised-floor buildings existed as early as the Early Jōmon period.{{Sfn|Ishino|2006|pp=187–189}}

== Forms ==

[[File:Ikegami-sone ruins04s3200.jpg|thumb|250px|right|Reconstruction of a raised-floor building in the "roof-storehouse" style at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site|1=Ikegami-Sone Site}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.]]

Raised-floor buildings are thought to have existed in two forms: one with walls above the floor, and another called "roof-storehouses" (屋根倉, *Yanegura*), where the roof (usually {{ill|Gable roof|en|Gable roof|WD=Q149617|lt=gabled|1=Gable roof}}) rests directly on the floor without walls.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}} The typical {{ill|raised-floor warehouse|ja|高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|2=ja|3=高床式倉庫|1=Raised floor building|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/raised-floor warehouse|13=User:Immanuelle/Raised floor building|qq=}} is reconstructed with walls, based on findings from sites like the {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro site|1=Toro (archaeological site)}} and {{ill|Yamaki site|ja|山木遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamaki site|1=Yamaki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11468753}} in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. Roof-storehouse style reconstructions can be seen in large posthole buildings at the {{ill|Ikegami-Sone Site|en|Ikegami-Sone Site|WD=Q11551440|lt=Ikegami-Sone Site|1=Ikegami-Sone Site}} spanning {{ill|Izumi, Osaka|en|Izumi, Osaka|WD=Q696412|lt=Izumi|1=Izumi, Osaka}} and {{ill|Izumiōtsu|en|Izumiōtsu|WD=Q873553|lt=Izumiotsu|1=Izumiōtsu}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}.{{Sfn|Agency for Cultural Affairs, Monuments Division|2013|p=165}}
